subject: Science

description: Draw conclusions and make predictions from picture graphs (e.g., make predictions based on weather data presented in a picture graph).

description: Understand how data can be used to make decisions.

subject: Science
description: Recognize that data can be retrieved from different computing devices (e.g., progress in a video game; pictures from a smartphone).

subject: Science

description: Discover how data can be stored in and retrieved from multiple locations.

subject: Science

description: Integrate constructive feedback while working in teams.
description_plain: Your child will listen to and use helpful advice from others when working in a group.

subject: Science
description: Set and implement equitable expectations and workloads when working in teams.
description_plain: Your child can help make sure everyone in the group has a fair share of the work and clear expectations.

subject: Science
description: Collaborate productively with individuals of varying perspectives, skills, and backgrounds.
description_plain: Your child will work well with people who have different ideas, skills, and backgrounds.

subject: Science
description: Select appropriate technological tools that can be used to collaborate on a project.
description_plain: Your child can choose the right technology tools to work together on a project.

subject: Science
description: Consider the needs of a variety of end users regarding accessibility and usability.
description_plain: Your child thinks about what different users need to make sure things are easy and fair for everyone to use.

subject: Science
description: Consider others' perspectives as well as one's own perspective when developing computational solutions.
description_plain: Your child considers other people's views as well as their own when creating computer-based solutions.

subject: Science
description: Recognize that equitable access to computing benefits society as a whole.
description_plain: Your child understands that making sure everyone can use computers fairly helps the whole community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Collaborate with others to identify a classroom or school issue and propose a resolution using civic dispositions.
description_plain: Your child works with classmates to find a problem at school and suggests a way to fix it by being a good citizen.

subject: Social Studies
description: Establish and practice classroom rules and procedures for listening and responding appropriately to others.
description_plain: Your child learns and practices classroom rules for listening and responding politely to others.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the purpose of rules and laws, and discuss consequences of breaking them.
description_plain: Your child understands why we have rules and laws and talks about what happens if someone breaks them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify similarities and differences between people and discuss ways to protect and respect all people by practicing civic dispositions.
description_plain: Your child notices how people are alike and different and learns how to respect and protect everyone.

subject: Social Studies
description: Utilize the college and career skills of a political scientist to understand and display civic dispositions in one's personal community.
description_plain: Your child uses skills like a political scientist to understand and show good citizenship in their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify an economic want or need at one's school or community level and create a solution.
description_plain: Your child finds a want or need at school or in the community and thinks of a way to solve it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why people have jobs, and describe the economic benefits for self and community.
description_plain: Your child learns why people have jobs and how jobs help themselves and their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how wants and needs change over time.
description_plain: Your child understands that what people want and need can change over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and compare wants and needs.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between wants and needs.

subject: Social Studies
description: Utilize the college and career skills of an economist to understand how economic decisions affect one's personal community.
description_plain: Your child uses skills like an economist to see how money choices affect their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe and compare the cultural and natural environment around one's home and school by constructing a visual representation.
description_plain: Your child describes and compares the natural and cultural surroundings near home and school by making a picture or map.

subject: Social Studies
description: Utilize sources of geographic information (e.g., digital sources, maps, or photographs/images) to define and identify cultural and/or natural features.
description_plain: Your child uses maps, photos, or digital tools to find and name natural and cultural features.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify a map, various map features, and explain the purpose of maps.
description_plain: Your child can identify a map, its parts, and explain why we use maps.

subject: Social Studies
description: Utilize the college and career skills of a geographer to apply map skills and draw conclusions about place in one's personal community.
description_plain: Your child uses skills like a geographer to read maps and learn about places in their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify different forms of evidence used in historical inquiry, such as digital sources, maps, photographs/images, or texts.
description_plain: Your child recognizes different types of evidence like maps, photos, or texts to learn about history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine ways in which individuals change or stay the same over time.
description_plain: Your child can notice how people change or stay the same as time passes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify similarities and differences between oneself and others.
description_plain: Your child can find ways they are like or different from other people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Utilize the college and career skills of a historian to study continuity and change over time for one's personal history and one's community.
description_plain: Your child uses historian skills to learn how their own life and community have changed or stayed the same over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Collaborate with others to identify, resolve, and communicate resolutions on a local or state issue.
description_plain: Your child works with others to find and share solutions to local or state problems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate ways to display active and responsible citizenship in local and state government.
description_plain: Your child shows how to be an active and responsible citizen in local and state government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the basic purpose, structure, and functions of South Carolina's government at both the local and state level.
description_plain: Your child can explain what South Carolina’s local and state governments do and how they are organized.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate how civic dispositions encourage citizens with diverse beliefs and backgrounds to work together for a common goal.
description_plain: Your child shows how people with different beliefs and backgrounds can work together to reach a shared goal.

subject: Social Studies
description: Utilize the college and career skills of a political scientist to understand and display civic dispositions about contemporary South Carolina.
description_plain: Your child uses political scientist skills to understand and show good citizenship in today’s South Carolina.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify an economic want or need at the local or state level and create a solution in the form of a good or a service.
description_plain: Your child can find a local or state need or want and think of a good or service to help meet it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Research and describe how goods and services differ in rural, suburban, and urban areas in South Carolina.
description_plain: Your child learns how goods and services are different in rural, suburban, and city areas of South Carolina.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how goods and services change over time.
description_plain: Your child explains how goods and services have changed over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare goods and services in the school, community, and state.
description_plain: Your child compares goods and services found at school, in the community, and in the state.

subject: Social Studies
description: Utilize the college and career skills of an economist to understand how economic decisions affect South Carolinians.
description_plain: Your child uses economist skills to understand how economic choices affect people in South Carolina.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe and compare various landforms within South Carolina through the use of primary and secondary sources.
description_plain: Your child describes and compares different landforms in South Carolina using pictures and information sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and differentiate between rural, suburban, and urban areas within South Carolina.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between rural, suburban, and city areas in South Carolina.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe the geographic location of South Carolina in relation to the rest of the United States through the use of various maps and geographic tools.
description_plain: Your child finds South Carolina on maps and describes where it is in the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify various types of maps, map features, and the purpose of maps.
description_plain: Your child knows different kinds of maps, their parts, and why people use maps.

subject: Social Studies
description: Utilize the college and career skills of a geographer to apply map skills and draw conclusions about places in South Carolina and South Carolina's place in the surrounding region.
description_plain: Your child uses geographer skills to read maps and understand places in South Carolina and nearby areas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate different sources of evidence used in historical inquiry, such as art, artifacts, digital sources, graphs, maps, oral histories, photographs/images, and texts.
description_plain: Your child looks at different sources like art, photos, and stories to learn about history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze a current event in South Carolina and make predictions about possible outcomes.
description_plain: Your child studies a current event in South Carolina and guesses what might happen next.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify similarities and differences between one's community and other South Carolina communities over time.
description_plain: Your child can find ways that their community is alike and different from other South Carolina communities now and in the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: Utilize the college and career skills of a historian to study continuity and change over time in South Carolina.
description_plain: Your child will use historian skills to study what has stayed the same and what has changed in South Carolina over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use evidence to propose and communicate a resolution to a national issue.
description_plain: Your child can use facts to suggest and explain a solution to a problem that affects the whole country.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how rights are granted to U. S. citizens through the founding documents.
description_plain: Your child understands how important U.S. documents give citizens their rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use primary and secondary sources to research a national figure who demonstrated civic dispositions.
description_plain: Your child can research a national leader who showed good citizenship by using different kinds of information sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify cultural and ethnic groups in the U. S., explore their characteristics, and communicate how civic dispositions build relationships between groups in a diverse society.
description_plain: Your child can name different cultural and ethnic groups in the U.S., learn about their traits, and explain how good citizenship helps people from different groups get along.

subject: Social Studies
description: Responsible citizenship requires individuals of diverse cultural backgrounds to employ dispositions that promote strong relationships to develop solutions to communal problems.
description_plain: Your child understands that being a responsible citizen means people from different backgrounds work together kindly to solve community problems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Interpret data to show how geographic location and available resources impact economic decision-making.
description_plain: Your child can read information to show how where people live and what resources they have affect their money choices.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create a simple budget, and articulate the priorities using economic terms such as expenses, income, and savings.
description_plain: Your child can make a simple budget and explain what is most important to spend money on using words like expenses, income, and savings.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how budgets change as wants and needs or the availability of goods and services change.
description_plain: Your child understands that budgets change when what people want or need changes or when goods and services are more or less available.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the purpose of currency and how income, savings, and spending are parts of a budget.
description_plain: Your child can explain why we use money and how earning, saving, and spending are parts of a budget.

subject: Social Studies
description: Utilize the college and career skills of an economist to understand how economic decisions affect citizenship within the United States.
description_plain: Your child will use economist skills to understand how money choices affect being a citizen in the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the distribution of human features, physical features, and natural resources within the U. S changes over time and impacts economic activity.
description_plain: Your child can explain how where people live, natural features, and resources in the U.S. change over time and affect the economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe and compare various landforms over time within the U.S. through the use of primary and secondary sources.
description_plain: Your child can describe and compare different landforms in the U.S. over time by using different sources like pictures and stories.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the geographic location of the U. S. in relation to the rest of the world.
description_plain: Your child can point out where the United States is located in the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Utilize the college and career skills of a geographer to apply map skills and draw conclusions about the United States.
description_plain: Your child will use map skills like a geographer to learn about and make conclusions about the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate different forms of evidence used in historical inquiry and determine their validity.
description_plain: Your child can look at different types of historical evidence and decide if they are trustworthy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze patterns of continuities and changes within U.S. history through the use of a variety of sources, including graphic organizers, maps, oral histories, photographs/images, texts, and timelines.
description_plain: Your child can study U.S. history by looking at maps, pictures, stories, timelines, and other sources to find what stayed the same and what changed.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine current or past events from U.S. history, and discuss the possible causes and effects.
description_plain: Your child can look at past or current U.S. events and talk about what might have caused them and what happened because of them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and compare significant historical events, moments, and symbols in U.S. history.
description_plain: Your child can name and compare important events, moments, and symbols in U.S. history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Utilize the college and career skills of a historian to study the continuity and changes over time in the United States.
description_plain: Your child can use historian skills to study what has stayed the same and what has changed over time in the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use maps and other geographic representations to identify how migration patterns affect people and places.
description_plain: Your child can use maps to show how people moving from place to place affects those places and the people living there.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate and explain the economic, social, political, and environmental motivations behind human migration and how places can change as a result.
description_plain: Your child understands why people move because of money, society, politics, and the environment, and how moving changes places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use maps and other geographic representations to identify exploration patterns throughout Earth history.
description_plain: Your child can use maps to find and explain patterns of exploration throughout Earth's history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate and explain the economic, social, and political motivations behind human exploration of Earth.
description_plain: Your child understands why people explore the Earth for reasons like money, society, and politics.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate an understanding of how and why humans have explored and migrated across Earth.
description_plain: Your child understands how and why people have explored and moved to different parts of the Earth.

subject: Social Studies
description: Research and create a geographic representation of a contemporary or historic group of people to communicate findings about their cultural characteristics and livelihoods.
description_plain: Your child can research a group of people, past or present, and create a map or picture to show their culture and how they live.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate the economic and land use characteristics of places and regions around the world.
description_plain: Your child investigates how people use land and earn money in different places around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate the cultural characteristics of places and regions around the world.
description_plain: Your child learns about the cultures of different places and regions around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate an understanding of varied human cultural and economic characteristics across Earth's surface.
description_plain: Your child understands that people have different cultures and ways of making a living in different parts of the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Develop a natural disaster safety plan for a community.
description_plain: Your child can create a safety plan to help a community prepare for natural disasters.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use maps and other sources of geographic information to gather evidence and draw conclusions about patterns of natural disasters around the world.
description_plain: Your child uses maps and other information to find patterns of natural disasters around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the range of natural hazards facing people and explain how some populations are more vulnerable than others.
description_plain: Your child knows about different natural dangers people face and understands why some groups are more at risk.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate an understanding of the relationship between Earth's environmental hazards and human activities.
description_plain: Your child understands how human activities and Earth's environmental dangers affect each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify spatial variations in climates around the world and recognize the relationship between climate and human activities.
description_plain: Your child can identify different climates around the world and explain how climate affects what people do.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and analyze the ways people interact with the physical environment in different regions of the state, the country, and the world.
description_plain: Your child learns how people interact with the land and environment in different parts of the state, country, and world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize and explain how physical features are distributed around the world.
description_plain: Your child can recognize and explain where physical features like mountains and rivers are found around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate an understanding of Earth's physical features and ecosystems that affect human activities.
description_plain: Your child understands how Earth's landforms and ecosystems influence what people do.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the spatial hierarchy of political and physical geographic features.
description_plain: Your child can identify how political and physical features are organized in different places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Locate the world's four hemispheres (i.e., northern, southern, eastern, and western) by using the major components of latitude and longitude (i.e., the Equator, the Prime Meridian, lines of latitude (i.e., parallels), lines of longitude (i.e., meridians), and the International Date Line).
description_plain: Your child can find the four hemispheres on a map using lines like the Equator and Prime Meridian.

subject: Social Studies
description: Utilize an alphanumeric grid to locate the continents and oceans.
description_plain: Your child can use a letter-and-number grid to find continents and oceans on a map.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use maps and globes to categorize places and regions by their human and physical conditions.
description_plain: Your child will use maps and globes to sort places by their natural features and how people live there.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple perspectives of the economic, political, and social effects of Reconstruction on different populations in the South and in other regions of the U. S.
description_plain: Your child understands how Reconstruction affected different groups of people in the South and other parts of the U.S. in economic, political, and social ways.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and evaluate the impact of economic, political, and social events on the African American experience throughout Reconstruction.
description_plain: Your child can identify how events during Reconstruction changed life for African Americans.

subject: Social Studies
description: Contextualize the economic, labor, political, and social conditions in South Carolina during the period of Reconstruction.
description_plain: Your child understands what life was like in South Carolina during Reconstruction, including work, politics, and society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Summarize Reconstruction as a turning point in American history.
description_plain: Your child can explain why Reconstruction was an important turning point in American history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the impact of federal legislation on the South during Reconstruction.
description_plain: Your child understands how laws passed by the federal government affected the South during Reconstruction.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the roles of various groups on Reconstruction.
description_plain: Your child can compare how different groups took part in Reconstruction.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate an understanding of the contributions different groups made to impact the economic, political, and social developments during Reconstruction of the United States and South Carolina in the period of 1860–1880.
description_plain: Your child understands how different groups helped shape the economy, politics, and society during Reconstruction in the U.S. and South Carolina from 1860 to 1880.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the economic, political, and social divisions during the Civil War.
description_plain: Your child can explain the economic, political, and social divisions that existed during the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and evaluate the economic, political, and social changes experienced throughout the Civil War.
description_plain: Your child can identify how the Civil War changed the economy, politics, and society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Contextualize South Carolina's experience during the Civil War.
description_plain: Your child understands what South Carolina experienced during the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how emancipation was achieved as a result of civic participation.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people working together helped end slavery.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the effects of military strategies utilized by the Union and the Confederacy.
description_plain: Your child understands how the military plans of the Union and Confederacy affected the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the economic and political causes of the Civil War.
description_plain: Your child can compare the economic and political reasons that caused the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate an understanding of economic, political, and social divisions during the United States Civil War, including the role of South Carolina between 1850–1870.
description_plain: Your child understands the economic, political, and social divisions during the Civil War, including South Carolina’s role from 1850 to 1870.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple perspectives of early westward expansion, including the addition of slave and free territories and states.
description_plain: Your child can explain different views about early westward expansion, including how new states were added as free or slave states.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize patterns of continuity and change in the experiences of Native Americans and Spanish-speaking people as the U. S. expanded westward.
description_plain: Your child recognizes how Native Americans and Spanish-speaking people’s lives stayed the same or changed as the U.S. grew west.

subject: Social Studies
description: Contextualize South Carolina's role in the development of sectionalism during the antebellum period.
description_plain: Your child understands South Carolina’s role in the growing divisions between regions before the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the role of technology and the environmental impact during the period of Westward Expansion.
description_plain: Your child can explain how new technology and changes to the environment affected westward expansion.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the effects of government policies in promoting United States territorial expansion into the west.
description_plain: Your child can explain how government decisions helped the United States grow bigger by moving west.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the motivations for and reactions to various expeditions into the Western territories.
description_plain: Your child can compare why people went on trips to the Western lands and how others reacted to those trips.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate an understanding of the expansion and growth of South Carolina and the United States between 1800–1850.
description_plain: Your child understands how South Carolina and the United States grew and changed between 1800 and 1850.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple perspectives on the economic, political, and social developments of the new nation.
description_plain: Your child can look at different points of view about the new country's money, government, and society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the continuities and changes in natural rights as seen from the French and Indian War to the creation of the Bill of Rights.
description_plain: Your child can explain what stayed the same and what changed about people's rights from the French and Indian War to the Bill of Rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: Contextualize South Carolina's role in the development of the new nation.
description_plain: Your child understands how South Carolina helped shape the new United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the sequence of events that led to the establishment of the U. S. as a democratic republic.
description_plain: Your child can explain the order of events that led to the United States becoming a democratic republic.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the economic and political motivations for colonists to declare independence from Great Britain.
description_plain: Your child can explain why colonists wanted to break away from Great Britain because of money and government reasons.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the roles of marginalized groups during the American Revolution.
description_plain: Your child can compare how different groups who were often left out played roles during the American Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate an understanding of the identity of a new nation, including the state of South Carolina between 1730-1800.
description_plain: Your child understands what made the new nation and South Carolina unique between 1730 and 1800.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple perspectives on the economic, political, and social developments of British North America and South Carolina.
description_plain: Your child can look at different views about the money, government, and society in British North America and South Carolina.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify patterns of change and continuity in the development of economic systems in British North America.
description_plain: Your child can find patterns of what changed and what stayed the same in how people made and used money in British North America.

subject: Social Studies
description: Contextualize the experience of Africans, Europeans, and Native Americans in South Carolina.
description_plain: Your child understands the experiences of Africans, Europeans, and Native Americans living in South Carolina.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the development of political institutions and social characteristics that defined the British colonial regions.
description_plain: Your child can explain how governments and social life developed in the British colonies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the effects of changing economic systems on the diverse populations in British North America.
description_plain: Your child can identify how changes in money systems affected different groups of people in British North America.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the interactions among cultural groups as a result of European colonization.
description_plain: Your child can compare how different cultural groups interacted because of European colonization.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate an understanding of the settlement and colonization of North America, including South Carolina, between 1600–1730.
description_plain: Your child understands how people settled and started colonies in North America, including South Carolina, between 1600 and 1730.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple perspectives on the economic, political, and social effects of global interdependence after 1980 using primary and secondary sources.
description_plain: Your child can look at different views about how countries around the world became connected after 1980 and how this affected money, government, and society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the continuities and changes in U.S. relationships with countries around the world as a result of the economic, political, and social changes in this period.
description_plain: Your child can explain what stayed the same and what changed in the United States’ relationships with other countries during this time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Contextualize the changes in rural communities in South Carolina within national and global industries.
description_plain: Your child understands how changes in South Carolina’s rural areas fit into bigger national and global industries.

subject: Social Studies
description: Summarize the global involvement of the U.S. using the fall of the Soviet Union as a turning point.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the U.S. became more involved in world events after the Soviet Union fell.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the impact of digital technologies on the U.S., and describe the impact those technologies had on its global influence.
description_plain: Your child understands how new digital technologies changed the U.S. and helped it have more influence around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the focus of the U.S. as a world leader before and after the September 11, 2001, attacks.
description_plain: Your child can tell how the U.S. focus as a world leader was different before and after the September 11, 2001 attacks.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate an understanding of the contemporary global economic, social, and political roles of the United States and South Carolina from 1980–present.
description_plain: Your child understands the important economic, social, and political roles the U.S. and South Carolina have played since 1980.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple perspectives on the economic, political, and social effects of the Cold War, Space Race, and Civil Rights Movement using primary and secondary sources.
description_plain: Your child can look at different points of view about how the Cold War, Space Race, and Civil Rights Movement affected people and society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the continuities and changes of race relations in the United States and South Carolina following the Supreme Court decisions of Briggs v. Elliott and Brown v. Board of Education.
description_plain: Your child understands how race relations in the U.S. and South Carolina changed or stayed the same after important court decisions about school segregation.

subject: Social Studies
description: Contextualize the tension between the United States and the Soviet Union during the Cold War.
description_plain: Your child can explain the reasons for the tension between the U.S. and the Soviet Union during the Cold War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Summarize the economic, political, and social changes in the U. S. after World War II.
description_plain: Your child can describe the economic, political, and social changes in the U.S. after World War II.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes and impacts of social movements in the U. S. and South Carolina.
description_plain: Your child understands why social movements started in the U.S. and South Carolina and what effects they had.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the capitalist and communist ideologies.
description_plain: Your child can explain the main differences between capitalism and communism.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate an understanding of the conflicts, innovations, and social changes in the United States, including South Carolina, from 1950–1980.
description_plain: Your child understands the conflicts, new inventions, and social changes in the U.S. and South Carolina from 1950 to 1980.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple perspectives on the economic, political, and social effects of World War II and its aftermath using primary and secondary sources.
description_plain: Your child can look at different views about how World War II and what happened after affected the economy, politics, and society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the changes and continuities regarding the United States' international leadership during the period, including the rebuilding of Europe and the resettlement of displaced persons resulting from the Holocaust.
description_plain: Your child understands how the U.S. stayed a world leader after World War II, including helping rebuild Europe and resettle people affected by the Holocaust.

subject: Social Studies
description: Contextualize the technological and geographic influence on military strategies in the Pacific and European theaters of war of World War II.
description_plain: Your child can explain how technology and geography affected military plans in the Pacific and Europe during World War II.

subject: Social Studies
description: Summarize the U.S. government's transition away from neutrality policies following World War I that led to its eventual involvement in World War II.
description_plain: Your child can summarize how the U.S. changed from staying neutral after World War I to joining World War II.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the cause and effect of government-sponsored policies within the United States and Europe related to the status of different groups, to include the Holocaust.
description_plain: Your child understands the causes and effects of government policies in the U.S. and Europe, including those related to the Holocaust.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the ideologies and policies that led to World War II.
description_plain: Your child can compare the ideas and policies that led to World War II.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate an understanding of the economic, political, and social effects of World War II, the Holocaust, and their aftermath (i.e., 1930–1950) on the United States and South Carolina.
description_plain: Your child understands how World War II, the Holocaust, and what happened afterward affected the U.S. and South Carolina between 1930 and 1950.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate multiple perspectives from the period, including the economic, political, and social impacts of World War I, the 1920s, the Great Depression, and the New Deal using primary and secondary sources.
description_plain: Your child can look at different views about how World War I, the 1920s, the Great Depression, and the New Deal affected the economy, politics, and society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the continuities and changes that resulted from New Deal programs and the impact these programs had on various groups throughout the U. S. and South Carolina.
description_plain: Your child understands how New Deal programs changed life for different groups in the U.S. and South Carolina.

subject: Social Studies
description: Contextualize the post-war economic climate on the cultural landscape throughout the United States and South Carolina.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the economy after the war affected the culture and daily life in the United States and South Carolina.

subject: Social Studies
description: Summarize how the role of the federal government expanded during the period.
description_plain: Your child can describe how the federal government took on more responsibilities during this time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the primary causes of World War I and the events which led to U.S. involvement.
description_plain: Your child can identify the main reasons World War I started and what events caused the U.S. to join the war.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the cultural and economic impacts of the 1929 Stock Market Crash on the U. S. and South Carolina.
description_plain: Your child can compare how the 1929 Stock Market Crash affected the economy and culture in both the U.S. and South Carolina.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate an understanding of how international events and conditions during the early 20th Century (i.e., 1910–1940) affected the United States and South Carolina.
description_plain: Your child understands how world events between 1910 and 1940 influenced life in the United States and South Carolina.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple perspectives on the economic, political, and social effects of western expansion, the Industrial Revolution, and immigration through primary and secondary sources, and evaluate the subsequent changes to the U. S.
description_plain: Your child can look at different viewpoints about how western expansion, the Industrial Revolution, and immigration changed the U.S. economically, politically, and socially.

subject: Social Studies
description: Summarize how imperialism and economic expansion impacted the experiences of different groups and shaped American cultural identities.
description_plain: Your child can explain how imperialism and economic growth affected different groups of people and shaped American culture.

subject: Social Studies
description: Contextualize how the Second Industrial Revolution led to an increased desire for raw materials and the United States involvement in imperialistic efforts and economic expansion.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Second Industrial Revolution increased the need for raw materials and led the U.S. to expand its economy and empire.

subject: Social Studies
description: Summarize how the United States' involvement in the Spanish American War led to increased U.S. economic expansion and imperialism.
description_plain: Your child can describe how the U.S. joining the Spanish American War helped the country grow economically and expand its influence.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine push- and pull-factors related to immigration and expansion on urban and rural populations during the period.
description_plain: Your child can explain why people moved to and from cities and rural areas during this time and how immigration affected these changes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the physical landscape and demographics of the U.S. before and after the Transcontinental Railroad.
description_plain: Your child can compare what the U.S. looked like and who lived there before and after the Transcontinental Railroad was built.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate an understanding of the economic, political, and social effects of expansion and industrialization on the United States and South Carolina between 1860–1910.
description_plain: Your child understands how expansion and industrial growth between 1860 and 1910 changed the economy, politics, and society in the U.S. and South Carolina.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple perspectives on global interdependence during the 20th and 21st centuries through a variety of primary and secondary sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the progression of technological developments and the resulting cultural diffusion throughout the 20th and 21st centuries.
description_plain: Your child can explain how new technologies spread and influenced cultures worldwide during the 20th and 21st centuries.

description: Analyze the impact of increased global interdependence using the Great Depression and Cold War as major turning points in the 20th century.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the impact of nationalism on global conflicts and genocides in the 20th and 21st centuries.
description_plain: Your child can explain how strong feelings of nationalism led to wars and genocides in the 20th and 21st centuries.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the global movements that resulted in the advancement or limitation of human rights during the 20th and 21st centuries.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate an understanding of the development of global interdependence from 1920 to the present.

subject: Science
description: Obtain and communicate information to define problems related to human impact on the local environment.
description_plain: Your child can find and share information about problems caused by people affecting the local environment.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions to understand the purpose of weather forecasting to prepare for and respond to severe weather.
description_plain: Your child asks questions to learn why weather forecasts help us get ready for bad weather.

subject: Science
description: Use a model to represent the relationship between the needs of different plants or animals (including humans)and the places they live.
description_plain: Your child will use a model to show how different plants and animals (including people) need certain things and places to live.

subject: Science
description: Construct an argument supported by evidence for how plants and animals (including humans) can change the environment to meet their needs.
description_plain: Your child can explain with examples how plants and animals (including people) change their surroundings to meet their needs.

subject: Science
description: Use and share observations of local weather conditions to describe patterns over time.
description_plain: Your child will watch and share what the weather is like in your area to notice patterns over time.

subject: Science
description: Use observations to describe patterns of what plants and animals (including humans) need to survive.
description_plain: Your child will watch and describe what plants and animals (including people) need to live and grow.

subject: Science
description: Use tools and materials to design and build a structure that will reduce the warming effect of sunlight on an area.
description_plain: Your child can use tools and materials to build something that helps keep an area cooler by blocking sunlight.

subject: Science
description: Make observations to determine the effect of sunlight on Earth's surface.
description_plain: Your child will observe and describe how sunlight affects the Earth's surface.

subject: Science
description: Analyze data to determine if a design solution works as intended to change the speed or direction of an object with a push or a pull.
description_plain: Your child can look at data to decide if a design works to change how fast or which way an object moves when pushed or pulled.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct an investigation to compare the effects of different strengths or different directions of pushes and pulls on the motion of an object.
description_plain: Your child will plan and do an experiment to see how different pushes and pulls affect how an object moves.

subject: Science
description: Make observations at different times of year to relate the amount of daylight to the time of year.
description_plain: Your child can watch how the amount of daylight changes during different times of the year.

subject: Science
description: Use observations of the sun, moon, and stars to describe patterns that can be predicted.
description_plain: Your child can observe the sun, moon, and stars to find patterns that happen again and again.

subject: Science
description: Make observations to support an evidence-based claim that most young are like, but not exactly like, their parents.
description_plain: Your child observes and explains that babies look like their parents but are not exactly the same.

subject: Science
description: Obtain information from multiple sources to determine patterns in parent and offspring behavior that help offspring survive.
description_plain: Your child gathers information to find patterns in how parents and their babies behave to help the babies survive.

subject: Science
description: Use materials to design a solution to a human problem by mimicking how plants and/or animals use their external parts to help them survive, grow, and meet their needs.
description_plain: Your child can use materials to create a solution to a problem by copying how plants or animals use their body parts to survive and grow.

subject: Science
description: Use tools and materials to design and build a device that uses light or sound to communicate over a distance.
description_plain: Your child uses tools and materials to build something that sends messages using light or sound over a distance.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct an investigation to determine the effect of placing objects made with different materials in the path of a beam of light.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do an experiment to see what happens when light shines on objects made of different materials.

subject: Science
description: Make observations to support an evidence-based claim that objects in darkness can be seen only when illuminated by light sources.
description_plain: Your child observes and explains that we can only see objects in the dark if there is some light shining on them.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct investigations to provide evidence that vibrating materials can make sound and that sound can make materials vibrate.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do experiments to show that vibrating things make sound and sound can make things vibrate.

subject: Science
description: Design solutions to address human impacts on natural resources in the local environment.
description_plain: Your child designs ways to fix problems caused by people that hurt natural resources nearby.

subject: Science
description: Obtain information to identify where water is found on Earth and that it can be solid or liquid.
description_plain: Your child can find out where water is on Earth and understand that water can be ice or liquid.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to represent the shapes and kinds of land and bodies of water in an area.
description_plain: Your child can create a model that shows the shapes and types of land and water in a certain place.

subject: Science
description: Compare multiple solutions designed to slow or prevent wind or water from changing the shape of the land.
description_plain: Your child can compare different ways people try to stop wind or water from changing the land’s shape.

subject: Science
description: Use information from several sources to provide evidence that Earth events can occur rapidly or slowly.
description_plain: Your child uses information from different places to show that Earth events can happen quickly or slowly.

subject: Science
description: Make observations of plants and animals to compare patterns of diversity within different habitats.
description_plain: Your child watches plants and animals to compare how many different kinds live in different places.

subject: Science
description: Develop a simple model that mimics the function of an animal in dispersing seeds or pollinating plants.
description_plain: Your child can make a simple model that shows how an animal helps spread seeds or pollinate plants.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct an investigation to determine what plants need to grow.
description_plain: Your child plans and does an experiment to find out what plants need to grow.

subject: Science
description: Construct an argument with evidence that some changes caused by heating or cooling can be reversed and some cannot.
description_plain: Your child can explain with evidence which changes caused by heating or cooling can be undone and which cannot.

subject: Science
description: Make observations to construct an evidence-based account of how an object made of a small set of pieces can be disassembled and made into a new object.
description_plain: Your child can watch and describe how an object made of a few parts can be taken apart and made into something new.

subject: Science
description: Analyze data obtained from tests to determine which materials have the best properties for an intended purpose.
description_plain: Your child looks at test results to decide which materials work best for a certain job.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct an investigation to describe and classify different kinds of materials by their observable properties.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do an experiment to describe and sort materials by what they look and feel like.

subject: Science
description: Make a claim about the effectiveness of a design solution that reduces the impacts of a weather related hazard.
description_plain: Your child makes a statement about how well a design helps reduce problems caused by bad weather.

subject: Science
description: Obtain and combine information to describe climate patterns in different regions of the world.
description_plain: Your child gathers and combines information to describe weather patterns in different parts of the world.

subject: Science
description: Represent data in tables and graphical displays of typical weather conditions during a particular season to identify patterns and make predictions.
description_plain: Your child shows weather data in tables and graphs to find patterns and make guesses about future weather.

subject: Science
description: Make a claim about the effectiveness of a solution to a problem caused when the environment changes and affects organisms living there.
description_plain: Your child explains how a solution helps fix problems when changes in the environment affect living things.

subject: Science
description: Construct an argument with evidence that in a particular habitat some organisms can thrive, struggle to survive, or fail to survive.
description_plain: Your child uses evidence to explain why some animals or plants do well, have a hard time, or cannot survive in a certain place.

subject: Science
description: Use evidence to construct an explanation for how the variations in traits among individuals of the same species may provide advantages in surviving and producing offspring.
description_plain: Your child can explain how differences in traits among animals or plants help them survive and have babies.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data from fossils to provide evidence of organisms and the environments in which they lived long ago.
description_plain: Your child can look at fossil data to understand what animals lived long ago and what their environments were like.

subject: Science
description: Use evidence to support the explanation that traits can be influenced by the environment.
description_plain: Your child can use evidence to show how the environment can change traits in living things.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to provide evidence that plants and animals have inherited traits that vary within a group of similar organisms.
description_plain: Your child can study data to show that plants and animals inherit traits that can be different within the same group.

subject: Science
description: Construct an argument that some animals form groups that help members survive.
description_plain: Your child can explain why some animals live in groups to help each other survive.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use models to describe how organisms change in predictable patterns during their unique and diverse life cycles.
description_plain: Your child can create and use models to show how living things grow and change in regular ways during their life cycles.

subject: Science
description: Develop possible solutions to a simple design problem by applying scientific ideas about magnets.
description_plain: Your child can come up with simple design ideas using what they know about magnets.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions to determine cause-and-effect relationships of electric interactions and magnetic interactions between two objects not in contact with each other.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions to find out how electric and magnetic forces work between objects that don’t touch.

subject: Science
description: Make observations and measurements of an object's motion to provide evidence that a pattern can be used to predict future motion.
description_plain: Your child can watch and measure how things move to find patterns that help predict future motion.

subject: Science
description: Plan and conduct an investigation to provide evidence of the effects of balanced and unbalanced forces on the motion of an object.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do an experiment to show how balanced and unbalanced forces change how things move.

subject: Science
description: Generate and compare multiple solutions to reduce the impacts of natural Earth processes on humans.
description_plain: Your child comes up with and compares different ideas to lessen how natural Earth events impact people.

subject: Science
description: Obtain and combine information to describe that energy and fuels are derived from natural resources and how their uses affect the environment.
description_plain: Your child will gather information to explain that energy and fuels come from natural resources and that using them can affect the environment.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data from maps to describe patterns of Earth's features.
description_plain: Your child looks at maps and data to describe patterns in Earth’s features.

subject: Science
description: Make observations and/or measurements to provide evidence of the effects of weathering or the rate of erosion by water, ice, wind, or vegetation.
description_plain: Your child observes or measures how weathering or erosion by water, ice, wind, or plants changes the land.

subject: Science
description: Identify evidence from patterns in rock formations and fossils in rock layers to support an explanation for changes in a landscape over time.
description_plain: Your child uses patterns in rocks and fossils to explain how a landscape has changed over time.

subject: Science
description: Use a model to describe that animals receive different types of information through their senses, process the information in their brain, and respond to the information in different ways.
description_plain: Your child uses a model to show how animals get information through their senses, process it in their brain, and respond in different ways.

subject: Science
description: Construct an argument that plants and animals have internal and external structures that function together in a system to support survival, growth, behavior, and reproduction.
description_plain: Your child explains how plants and animals have body parts inside and outside that help them survive, grow, behave, and reproduce.

subject: Science
description: Generate and compare multiple solutions that use patterns to transmit information.
description_plain: Your child can create and compare different ways to send information using patterns.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe that light reflecting from objects and entering the eye allows objects to be seen.
description_plain: Your child makes a model to show that we see objects because light reflects off them and enters our eyes.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model of waves to describe patterns in terms of amplitude and wavelength and that waves can cause objects to move.
description_plain: Your child can build a simple model to show how waves move, including their height and length, and explain how waves can make things move.

subject: Science
description: Apply scientific ideas to design, test, and refine a device that converts energy from one form to another.
description_plain: Your child can design, test, and improve a device that changes energy from one type to another.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions and predict outcomes about the changes in energy that occur when objects collide.
description_plain: Your child will ask questions and guess what will happen to energy when objects bump into each other.

subject: Science
description: Make observations to provide evidence that energy can be transferred from place to place by sound, light, heat, and electric currents.
description_plain: Your child can watch and describe how energy moves from one place to another through sound, light, heat, and electricity.

subject: Science
description: Use evidence to construct an explanation relating the speed of an object to the energy of that object.
description_plain: Your child will use evidence to explain how the speed of something relates to its energy.

subject: Science
description: Evaluate potential solutions to problems that individual communities face in protecting the Earth's resources and environment.
description_plain: Your child can think about and judge different ways communities can protect Earth’s resources and environment.

subject: Science
description: Describe and graph the amounts of saltwater and fresh water in various reservoirs to provide evidence about the distribution of water on Earth.
description_plain: Your child can describe and graph how much saltwater and freshwater are in different places on Earth to show how water is spread out.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model using an example to describe ways the geosphere, biosphere, hydrosphere, and/or atmosphere interact.
description_plain: Your child can build a model to show how land, living things, water, and air work together.

subject: Science
description: Represent data in graphical displays to reveal patterns of daily changes in length and direction of shadows, day and night, and the seasonal appearance of some stars in the night sky.
description_plain: Your child can create graphs to show daily changes in shadows, day and night, and how some stars appear differently during seasons.

subject: Science
description: Support an argument with evidence that the apparent brightness of the sun compared to other stars is due to their relative distances from Earth.
description_plain: Your child can explain with evidence why the sun looks brighter than other stars because of how far away they are.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe the movement of matter among plants, animals, decomposers, and the environment.
description_plain: Your child can create a model to show how matter moves between plants, animals, decomposers, and the environment.

subject: Science
description: Support an argument with evidence that plants obtain materials they need for growth mainly from air and water.
description_plain: Your child can explain with evidence that plants get most of what they need to grow from air and water.

subject: Science
description: Use models to describe that energy in animals' food (used for body repair, growth, motion, and to maintain body warmth) was once energy from the sun.
description_plain: Your child can use models to show that the energy animals get from food originally comes from the sun.

subject: Science
description: Support an argument that the gravitational force exerted by Earth on objects is directed down.
description_plain: Your child can explain that Earth's gravity pulls objects straight down.

subject: Science
description: Conduct an investigation to determine whether the mixing of two or more substances results in new substances.
description_plain: Your child can do an experiment to find out if mixing substances creates new substances.

subject: Science
description: Make observations and measurements to identify materials based on their properties.
description_plain: Your child can observe and measure materials to identify them by their properties.

subject: Science
description: Measure and graph quantities to provide evidence that regardless of the type of change that occurs when heating, cooling, or mixing substances, the total weight of matter is conserved.
description_plain: Your child can measure and graph amounts to show that matter's weight stays the same during heating, cooling, or mixing.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe that matter is made of particles too small to be seen.
description_plain: Your child can create a model to show that matter is made of tiny particles too small to see.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data on natural hazards to identify patterns, which help forecast future catastrophic events and inform the development of technologies to mitigate their effects.
description_plain: Your child can study data about natural disasters to find patterns that help predict future events and create ways to reduce damage.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use models to describe how unequal heating and rotation of the Earth cause patterns of atmospheric and oceanic circulation that determine regional climates.
description_plain: Your child can build and use models to show how uneven heating and Earth’s spinning cause air and ocean movements that affect climate.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data to provide evidence for how the motions and complex interactions of air masses result in changes in weather conditions.
description_plain: Your child can study data to explain how moving air masses cause changes in weather.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe the cycling of water through Earth's systems driven by energy from the sun and the force of gravity.
description_plain: Your child can create a model to show how water moves through Earth's systems using energy from the sun and gravity.

subject: Science
description: Analyze and interpret data on the distribution of fossils and rocks, continental shapes, and seafloor structures to provide evidence of the past plate motions.
description_plain: Your child can look at fossils, rocks, and land shapes to find evidence of how Earth’s plates have moved in the past.

subject: Science
description: Construct an explanation based on evidence for how geoscience processes have changed Earth's surface at varying time and spatial scales.
description_plain: Your child can explain how Earth’s surface has changed over time by looking at evidence from Earth science.

subject: Science
description: Develop a model to describe the cycling of Earth's materials and the flow of energy that drives this process.
description_plain: Your child will build a model to show how Earth's materials move and how energy makes this happen.

subject: Science
description: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence from rock strata for how the geologic time scale is used to organize Earth's 4.6-billion-year-old history.
description_plain: Your child can explain how scientists use layers of rock to organize Earth’s 4.6-billion-year history.

subject: Science
description: Gather and synthesize information that sensory receptors respond to stimuli by sending messages to the brain for immediate behavior or storage as memories.
description_plain: Your child can gather and combine information to show how senses send messages to the brain to cause quick actions or memories.

subject: Science
description: Use argument supported by evidence for how the body is a system of interacting subsystems composed of groups of cells.
description_plain: Your child can explain, with evidence, how the body works as a system made of smaller parts called cells.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model to describe the function of a cell as a whole and ways the parts of cells contribute to the function.
description_plain: Your child can build and use a model to show how a cell works and how its parts help it do its job.

subject: Science
description: Conduct an investigation to provide evidence that living things are made of cells; either one cell or many different numbers and types of cells.
description_plain: Your child can do an investigation to show that all living things are made of cells, either one cell or many different kinds.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model to describe that waves are reflected, absorbed, or transmitted through various materials.
description_plain: Your child can build and use a model to show how waves can bounce off, go through, or be absorbed by different materials.

subject: Science
description: Plan an investigation to determine the relationships among the energy transferred, the type of matter, the mass, and the change in the average kinetic energy of the particles as measured by the temperature of the sample.
description_plain: Your child can plan an experiment to find out how energy transfer, type of matter, mass, and temperature changes are related.

subject: Science
description: Apply scientific principles to design, construct, and test a device that either minimizes or maximizes thermal energy transfer.
description_plain: Your child can design, build, and test a device that either reduces or increases heat transfer.

subject: Science
description: Develop and use a model that predicts and describes changes in particle motion, temperature, and state of a pure substance when thermal energy is added or removed.
description_plain: Your child can create a simple model to show how particles move, temperature changes, and states of matter change when heat is added or taken away.

subject: Science
description: Ask questions to clarify evidence of the factors that have impacted global temperatures over the past century.
description_plain: Your child will ask questions to understand what has caused changes in Earth's temperature over the last 100 years.

subject: Science
description: Construct an argument supported by evidence for how increases in human population and per-capita consumption of natural resources impact Earth's systems.
description_plain: Your child can explain, using evidence, how more people and using more natural resources affect Earth's systems.

subject: Science
description: Apply scientific principles to design a method for monitoring and minimizing a human impact on the environment.
description_plain: Your child will use science ideas to design a way to watch and reduce how people harm the environment.

subject: Science
description: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence for how the uneven distributions of Earth's mineral, energy, and groundwater resources are the result of past and current geoscience processes.
description_plain: Your child can explain, using evidence, how Earth's uneven spread of minerals, energy, and water comes from past and current Earth processes.

subject: Science
